data.title: Wayfair: Making Incremental Cost Improvements In A Flat Revenue Environment
data.description:
Wayfair (NYSE:W) reported Q4 2024 results that were slightly better than expected in terms of net revenue, although with adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow that was a bit lower than expected due to heavier advertising spend. Some of this advertising spend affects future quarters, so overall I'd consider Wayfair's Q4 2024 results as meeting expectations.

Wayfair is also exiting Germany, which should result in a modest bottom-line improvement going forward. It struggled to gain market share in Germany despite entering the market there in 2009.

I've increased my estimate of Wayfair's intrinsic value by $2 per share to $27 per share to reflect the positive impact of its exit from Germany. Other than that, Wayfair's results appear to be largely tracking to my prior expectations.

German Exit

Wayfair announced its decision to exit the German market in January 2025. This affected approximately 730 employees (around 3% of Wayfair's global workforce), although Wayfair expects around half of those positions to be relocated to other corporate offices.

Due to its exit from Germany, Wayfair expects total charges of around $102 million to $111 million, of which $40 million to $44 million is employee-related costs such as severance. The other $62 million to $67 million is primarily non-cash charges.

Wayfair entered the German market in 2009 (not long after it entered the UK), but it has had more success gaining market share in the UK. Germany accounts for a low-single digit percentage of Wayfair's revenues, and this move should help its profitability results a bit.

Q4 2024 Results

Wayfair ended up with $3.121 billion in Q4 2024 net revenues, +0.2% compared to Q4 2023. This was a bit better than its expectations for a low-single-digit decline during the quarter and was driven by its US business, which was up +1.1%.

Wayfair's gross margins were within expectations for the quarter, but it did report relatively high advertising expenses at 13.7% of net revenue. This was around 1% higher (as a percentage of net revenue) than Wayfair's prior guidance for advertising expense during the quarter.

The higher advertising costs kept Wayfair from improving its adjusted EBITDA significantly compared to Q4 2023, despite the better than expected sales.

Wayfair reported $96 million in adjusted EBITDA in Q4 2024, bringing its full-year total to $453 million. This was within my expected range of $450 million to $475 million in 2024 adjusted EBITDA, although towards the lower end of that range.

The effect of seasonal working capital changes in Q4 2024 increased Wayfair's free cash flow to $102 million for the quarter. This compares to $62 million in Q4 2023.

Wayfair reported $83 million in free cash flow for the full-year 2024, up from negative $2 million in 2023. This was slightly lower than my expectation for approximately $100 million in 2024 free cash flow.

If around half of the increased advertising expense in Q4 2024 was for impacting future quarters, then one could say that Wayfair's free cash flow and adjusted EBITDA met my expectations, though.

Wayfair reported $411 million in equity compensation and related taxes in 2024, down from $623 million in 2023. Wayfair's free cash flow would have been significantly negative if that compensation was paid in cash instead.

Equity compensation is also excluded from adjusted EBITDA, so Wayfair's adjusted EBITDA would have been only slightly positive (around $42 million) in 2024 if it was included in that calculation.

Potential 2025 Outlook

Wayfair provided some guidance notes for Q1 2025 where it expected net revenues to be flat to slightly down for the quarter. Around 100 basis points of impact for the quarter come from its exit from Germany, so without that move, it sounds like sales expectations may have been flat.

Other metrics such as gross margin and customer service and merchant fees are expected to be around typical levels, while Wayfair expects advertising to come down slightly from Q4 2024 levels after boosting advertising last quarter.

Wayfair also expects some continued improvement in selling, operations, technology, and G&A expenses, so its adjusted EBITDA should be similar to slightly improved from Q1 2024's $75 million. Around $75 million to $100 million would be reasonable to expect for Q1 2025.

While Wayfair hasn't provided formal full-year guidance, a similar scenario of roughly flat net revenue growth (excluding the impact of the German exit) and slightly improved adjusted EBITDA (through cost trimming) should be reasonable to expect.

Wayfair may thus be able to deliver $500 million to $550 million in adjusted EBITDA for 2025.

Notes On Valuation

My prior estimate of Wayfair's intrinsic value was $25 per share. This was based on it potentially getting to around $225 million per year in free cash flow by 2026.

Wayfair's Q4 2024 results and Q1 2025 outlook were largely in line with my expectations, but I've increased my estimate of Wayfair's intrinsic value by $2 per share to $27 per share. This reflects the expected positive impact of its Germany exit on its free cash flow.

Wayfair has been making some progress with cutting costs. However, a story of flat revenue growth plus some cost-cutting isn't all that exciting.

Wayfair's net revenues peaked in 2020 and since then, its net revenues have declined for several years. The decline in the past couple years hasn't been too much, at -2% in 2023 and -1% in 2024. Wayfair's net revenue growth for 2025 may be flat excluding Germany.

It should be noted that Wayfair's free cash flow numbers do not include the impact of its large amount of equity compensation. My $27 per share estimate of its intrinsic value is probably actually a bit on the generous side, given its large amount of ongoing equity compensation.

Conclusion

Wayfair has settled into a position where it can deliver roughly flat net revenue growth (excluding exits from markets) while modestly improving its adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow through cost-cutting.

At close to $42 per share, though, Wayfair is still trading at well above my estimate of its intrinsic value. I estimate its intrinsic value at $27 per share, a $2 increase from before due to the bottom-line improvements expected from its Germany exit.

Wayfair shouldn't really be considered a growth company, given that its net revenue peaked in 2020 and has gone down at least a little in every year since then. If Wayfair was a brick-and-mortar retailer with similar financial results, it would probably be trading at a much lower price than $42 per share.

data.title: QQQ Correction Phase In Progress? Follow The Volume (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
It's a rather straightforward idea. Markets tend to top on low volume and low volatility. The first sign of trouble is usually a high volume down day. We experienced both last week, especially in the Big Tech area of Wall Street.

The base logic is buying pressure becomes exhausted after everyone who wants to invest, piles into the market. Then a period of slower price movement to new highs takes place over weeks, while ultra-low volume pinpoints buying pressure is ebbing. All you need is an excuse to sell, where a number of large institutions and funds unload positions trying to lock-in gains while the getting is good. A rolling snowball effect begins at the top of the mountain, with selling speed and size jumping over time. This is how market meltdowns play out. The average retail investor usually holds on for a while, waiting to sell until the snowball is ruining the wealth of millions of hot-money investors, as price drops are headlining the nightly news on TV.

In my trading experience over 39 years, when market declines lead the national nightly news for a day or two, the emotional toll of holding losses becomes unbearable for many individuals. Such is often a smart time to go long, conversely in a contrarian manner, and purchase shares from late-to-the-game sellers.

The popular Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (NASDAQ:QQQ) provides a prime example of brewing trouble, perhaps with a sell signal emitted on Thursday and Friday (February 20th-21st), as price declined on heavy volume. Let me explain.

February's Topping Pattern

Today's QQQ pattern looks very familiar, rhyming with the December and July peaks for price and volume characteristics. I have boxed in gold a period of several weeks of exceptionally low volume/volatility together with new price highs, where one (or more) large-volume down day(s) started -7% and -10% selloffs, respectively. I am including another technical filter to eliminate times when volume fluctuates, but price does not, during an uptrend. The 20-day exponential moving average (EMV) is useful in this regard.

My red arrows highlight an uptick in volume selling that pushes price meaningfully lower, right after a period of slack volume. Lastly, I have drawn On Balance Volume changes at the bottom of my chart. Weaker OBV trends like January-February 2025 can explain a difficult demand environment that needs either a price correction or time before a meaningful upmove can resume.

Past Examples

The best way to explain the typical topping process in an index ETF is by showing some past examples with characteristics mirroring today's market. I will say the August 2023 peak (before a -10% correction) and late 2018 top (going into a -20% bear market) did not show the usual low-volume warnings like December 2024 and February 2025. Going back seven years, the following are the closest QQQ experiences vs. the current setup.

Second Half 2021

My first example is taken from the last major market top in late 2021. A cluster of low volume/volatility periods popped between September and December of that year. The September signal came in the middle of a -7% drop, while the November signal came at the final peak, with a separate confirming signal the first week of January (kicking off a significant market -30% decline during 2022). Note: The late December price move was similar to a double top formation. Very weak OBV readings were part of the December 2021 technical equation, which may mimic the last few weeks of trading in 2025.

Late 2020 - Early 2021

We can next review several -10% corrections in September 2020 and February-March 2021. You will notice very low volume alongside new highs in the market during late November and December 2020 did not witness a serious price retreat under the 20-day EMV.

2019 - Early 2020 Pandemic

Lastly, July-August 2019 experienced an -8% correction, following a clear low-volume warning for five weeks. Despite prolonged low-volume and low-volatility characteristics between November 2019 and early January 2020, price would not fall below the 20-day EMV until the last week of February 2020. Essentially, the COVID outbreak became breaking news. QQQ would sink rapidly by around -30% peak to trough (into late March).

Final Thoughts

I will say QQQ's chart setup is not unique. Other U.S. index ETFs have outlined low volume/volatility patterns during February, with Friday witnessing oversized selling volumes. This group of trading/investing products includes the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), SPDR Dow Jones Industrial Average ETF Trust (DIA), and many others. I would gauge QQQ as having the clearest bearish pattern.

If you are bullish on the market and Big Tech names, you want QQQ to jump back above its 20-day EMV. Such a development would negate the sell signal, at least over the short term.

You can experience a major top on high-volume up days. However, this is an atypical setup.

Does the low-volume "correlation" with price peaks tell us how far down future mass liquidations can push price? No. However, I remain skittish about the market's technical and fundamental setup. I was very bearish in early December, and we did get a minor -7% QQQ correction into the second week of January.

The Wall Street move higher over the last 4–5 weeks has been amazingly substandard for strength, with various advance/decline lines failing to confirm the NASDAQ 100 and S&P 500 new all-times in February. Euphoric call-option buying is still evident in the statistics, which is a worrisome red flag.

The stock market is amazingly overvalued on price to sales, the Shiller P/E Ratio, and total market value vs. GDP calculations (commonly referred to as the Buffett Indicator). You could argue the 2025 market condition is the most overvalued in all of American business history, worse than the 1920s and 1990s bubble blowing.

Now, we have tariffs (some enacted, some promised) and major fiscal cost-cutting throwing the economy a curveball.

My primary macroeconomic worry for 2025 revolves around the potential for major capital flight out of the U.S. dollar and our financial markets by foreign investors. With an estimated $20 to $30 trillion in U.S. assets owned by overseas entities, if Trump shakes the tree hard enough, we risk retaliation effects through the unintended consequence of oversized sell orders in bonds, stocks, and real estate holdings.

So, given the right circumstances, the downside in QQQ could be unusually large this year. For now, I am thinking a normal -10% correction could be next into the end of March (QQQ is down almost -3% already this week). Could this morph into a bear market drop of -20%? Absolutely. If Trump follows through on tariff threats, including imposing new import taxes on our international allies and friends, they may recall trillions in dollar investments as a knee-jerk reaction. Then, we will have serious and immediate problems financing $36 trillion in Treasury debt, as more speculative assets like cryptos and equities likely succumb to huge supply/demand problems.

I continue to suggest readers stay well diversified with financial portfolio assets such as gold/silver, cash, short-term bonds, inverse/short index ETF products, and select foreign equities, all part of the investment formula for long-term success. The goal is to reduce the risk of a major stock/bond decline hitting your net worth this year.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: The Cheesecake Factory: Good Results, But I'm Not A Buyer At This Price
data.description:
Cheesecake Factory is More of the Same, but Flower Child Impresses

The Cheesecake Factory (NASDAQ:CAKE) is baked to perfection! A double beat of EPS and revenue, yet the market’s reaction stays flat, already priced into the batter.

All joking aside, this casual dining restaurant chain has once again managed to offset the negative impacts of traffic through menu price increases. Meanwhile, its other concepts—like Flower Child and North Italia—continue to outperform both the fast-casual and casual dining segments.

However, I still have a question in my mind: Does Cheesecake Factory have significant upside ahead of it? I'll tell you soon.

Turning to earnings, Cheesecake Factory saw its same-store sales climb again during Q4. Granted, it was a bit of a tougher climb than it was earlier in the year, with same-store sales up 1.7% year-over-year, a 10 basis point sequential improvement.

Note that the bulk of these gains are still coming from a higher average check per guest. During the quarter, same-store sales grew 2.2% year-over-year, driven by a 4.2% year-over-year price increase and a negative 2% year-over-year mix. Traffic was -0.4% year-over-year, improving 40 basis points sequentially from Q3. This was predictable, as we’ve seen before that Q4 was a breather for operators amid a year of stagnant same-store sales.

Both North Italia and Flower Child saw their same-store sales grow. Of course, each in its own way. While the Italian-American restaurant chain saw its same-store sales grow 1% year-over-year, the trendy fast-casual chain saw them grow 11% year-over-year.

What a blow! This is a huge win for the brand, and it’s sure to excite the spinoff-supporters.

Just like Cheesecake Factory, North Italia saw a 2% price roll-over in October, which ended up offsetting traffic, which was most likely negative during the quarter.

Before we move on, I'd like to show how this has played out over the past few quarters. I don't have any compiled data for Flower Child:

Add that to the nine restaurants opened during Q4 FY 2024, and you see top-line sales of $921 million during the quarter. That represents 5% year-over-year growth, below the company’s growth algorithm of 8%. For all of FY 2024, we saw top-line sales of $3.581 billion, growing 4.1% year-over-year.

If we break this down, you’ll see that Cheesecake Factory is growing much slower than the other concepts. Take a look at how this played out during FY 2024:

  • The Cheesecake Factory: $669.4 million, representing 72.7% of total revenues during Q4. They grew 2% year-over-year;
  • North Italia: $81.3 million, representing 8.8% of total revenues during Q4. They grew 21% year-over-year;
  • Flower Child: $38.2 million, representing 4.1% of total revenues during Q4. They grew 25% year-over-year.

The remainder belongs to other concepts under the Fox Restaurant Concepts umbrella, which grew 20% year-over-year during Q4. Note that all of the other concepts are growing much faster than Cheesecake Factory.

Knowing this, the company should accelerate new openings, especially for Flower Child, which already fits the thesis of high-growth fast-casuals that serve healthy food, such as CAVA (CAVA) and Sweetgreen (SG). Note the size of the addressable market for the three brands:

Notably, we already have a few openings in FY 2025: 1 North Italia, 2 Flower Child and 2 FRC restaurants. By the end of Q1, at least three more brand-new restaurants will be set to open. Now, do you notice that they are accelerating the growth brands in their portfolio? Smart move.

Flower Child locations are much cheaper to build.

If you look closely, even if they don’t open as many Cheesecake Factory locations, the brand should continue to act as the cash cow in their portfolio. It generates AUVs of $12.5 million. That’s impressive, but it seems more advantageous to ride the rise in fast-casual popularity with Flower Child.

Let me explain. With AUVs of $4.5 million and construction costs of approximately $2 million. Sure, Cheesecake Factory's margins are still higher (18.4%), but fast-casual generates a much more interesting payback and can scale much faster. Pay attention:

  • For Flower Child, I assume pre-opening costs of $0.3 to $0.5 million and equipment costs of $0.5 to $1 million, construction cost is estimated at $1.5 to $1.75 million, averaging $2 million. This seems fair to me for 3,500 square foot size boxes. Note that even though they seem cheap, they are still not as scalable as Wingstop (WING) or Potbelly (PBPB), which are fast-casuals that aim for lower construction costs due to franchising. However, when we compare them to Shake Shack (SHAK), which has a greater concentration in its corporate store portfolio, we notice some similarity. Compare them to the sumptuous Cheesecake Factory restaurants and you will see the difference. They cost between $8 to $10 million. I will use $9 million for our study;
  • Cash flow by location is $2.3 million for Cheesecake Factory (restaurant-level margins of 18.4%) and $738,000 for Flower Child (restaurant-level margins of 16.4%);
  • The payback period for Cheesecake Factory restaurants is 4 to 5 years. Note that even with industry-leading AUVs and healthy restaurant-level margins, the average 8,750 square foot locations are expensive. For Flower Child, the payback period can be as short as two years if sales at the location are strong;
  • The Cost-Benefit Index is 4.2 for Cheesecake Factory locations, while it is 6.39 for Flower Child locations. The IRR also shows an analogous situation, with Cheesecake Factory locations being 23.5% and Flower Child locations being 35.5%.

You might be thinking that Cheesecake Factory's numbers are bad, right? Quite the contrary. Flower Child's numbers are really good. Compare Cheesecake Factory's numbers to other restaurants in the casual dining segment, and you'll see that they're above average.

New Menu: Adding More Dishes Is Playing With Fire

For the Cheesecake Factory, having a large menu is never enough. Going against the grain of the industry, which has been cutting back on low-margin or even low-performing dishes, as Chili's (EAT) did in its epic turnaround, Cheesecake has been continually adding new menu items.

Its book-like menu has about 250 items to suit all tastes. It started small in 1978 when David Overton started in Beverly Hills. Time went by and from sandwiches, burgers and cheesecakes, Overton served "everything America wanted".

To give you an idea, the waiters are trained for two full weeks until they memorize the menu. The cooks suffer a little more. It takes three to four weeks to learn everything. Of course, there is a division of labor according to each chef's specialties (pasta, red meat, seafood, etc.) that Adam Smith would be proud of. Come on, at the end of the day, there are seven hundred ingredients! I got tired just thinking about it.

This new update adds a smash burger, vegetarian options, roasted carrots, Asian pickles, and jalapeño chicken tenders. The menu items range in price from $12.95 to $31. This is slightly above the entry-level prices of FSRs, especially those that are offering value deals like Chili's, BJ's Restaurants (BJRI), Applebee's (DIN), and many others.

I miss a meal-combo platform here. Maybe that would be the nudge needed to change the downward trajectory of transactions.

Keeping an Eye on the Finances of This FSR

As I mentioned, The Cheesecake Factory ended FY 2024 with top-line sales of approximately $3.581 billion, continuing the sales growth trend since it acquired FRC in late FY 2019. Shortly thereafter, sales were impacted by the pandemic, but have since grown with the expansion of other satellite concepts.

Looking at the margins, they seem to have returned to levels similar to those of the first half of the last decade. See how they behaved below:

To improve the margin picture over the past five years, The Cheesecake Factory has seen its operating costs decline as it has started to scale sales again. That’s what we’ve seen with FRC as well. When it operated concepts like North Italia and Flower Child, it suffered from higher operating costs and stagnant top-line sales. Now that it’s slowly growing, its margins are improving.

However, compared to the middle of the last decade, Cheesecake Factory is no longer a restaurant chain with strong EBITDA and EBIT generation. Other operating expenses and a relatively higher SG&A compared to revenue are the culprits here. But I believe the curve has inverted during FY 2022, with Cheesecake Factory achieving margins similar to pre-pandemic ones in the coming years.

Something similar can be seen from OCF and FCF. OCF grew 22.8% year over year to $268.3 million, reaching similar levels from FY 2015 to FY 2018. FCF did not reach this level, but remains one of the highest since the post-pandemic period at $108 million. Likewise, the need for investment is the highest since FY 2015, encompassing the opening of more locations year over year, mainly from Flower Child and North Italia.

This stronger FCF allows Cheesecake Factory to better handle debt maturities, even deleveraging its balance sheet if it chooses to do so. However, its long-term debt has remained virtually unchanged since then at $470 million.

This is not a problem, as the company maintains a Debt/EBITDA ratio of 1.59x, which is more than reasonable for its size and cash flow generation. I wonder if Cheesecake Factory had not issued any equity during FY 2021 and had at least $100 million more in long-term debt, would the shareholder returns have been more consistent? We would still have a debt/EBITDA ratio of less than 2x and shares outstanding would remain in the high-40 million range.

Regarding liquidity, I don't see many problems ahead. The company has $341 million in total liquidity available, including a cash balance of $84 million. While we have FCF being generated, debts can be refinanced without much problem. They can even reduce it at the same time if that is what management wants.

Other than that, I don't see any significant increase in operating assets that could impact investment decisions. In fact, Cheesecake Factory can finance them through its operating liabilities.

Finally, let's take a look at the guidance for the entire FY 2025:

  1. Top-line sales of $3.8 billion. This represents at least 8.6% year-over-year growth, in line with the company's projected long-term growth algorithm;
  2. Net profit margin of 4.75% ($180.5 million). Note that this would mean a 15.1% year-over-year increase in net income. On the projected margin, it represents a slight expansion from FY 2024, approaching pre-pandemic levels;
  3. Opening 25 new restaurants in 2025, with up to 15 openings in the first half of the year and the remainder in the second half. This includes up to 3-4 Cheesecake Factories, 6-7 North Italias, 6-7 Flower Childs, and 8-9 FRC restaurants. Note that this is consistent with what we saw in our unit-level economic feasibility analysis, they are opening more satellite concepts than ever before. Capex for these openings would be between $190 million and $210 million, at least 18.8% higher year-over-year than it was in FY 2024.

Trying to Find a PT Based on Guidance Predictions

Those of you readers with slightly better memories than I do may recall that I had a $46 PT on Cheesecake Factory. Now, as a value investor, I like to establish a proper margin of safety before buying (or recommending) stocks, so I preferred to maintain a 'Hold' recommendation on Cheesecake Factory before.

However, things have changed. They beat expectations and released very interesting guidance for FY 2025. I feel like I should try to update my PT on Cheesecake Factory based on these company forecasts. Let's see if my $46 PT has aged well or if we will change it.

I'll start with the NPVGO Model. Just to make my assumptions clear here:

  1. I'll assume EPS for FY 2025 to be $3.54, based on the net income and shares outstanding forecasts for Cheesecake Factory;
  2. For the payout ratio, I will use the analyst consensus estimate for FY 2025 ($1.10). This gives us a rate of approximately 31%;
  3. For ROA, I am considering projected net income and assets of $3.125 billion. This gives us a projected ROA of 5.7%;
  4. I will also use a WACC of 6.4%.

Based on these assumptions, we get an IGR of 3.93%. If Cheesecake Factory retains $2.44, it would grow by $0.14 every year. This would give us an NPV from retention of -$0.27. Carrying this out into perpetuity, we find an NPVGO of -$10.83. The value of the stock would be what it would be if it were a cash cow and distributed all of its earnings ($55.31) minus the NPVGO. This situation would give us a PT of $44.48.

Considering that Cheesecake Factory has issued conservative guidance for FY 2025, we have a consensus EPS estimate of $3.71. Now, that would give us a payout of 29% and an ROA of 6.04%. Note that this would give us a PT of $50.95.

If you're even more optimistic, we have the highest projection of an EPS of $3.85. Now, that would give us a payout of 28% and an ROA of 6.25%. That would give us a PT of $56.74.

Notice that in all three projections, Cheesecake Factory's price is at most fairly valued. You can consider an average of the three models. This would give us a PT of approximately$50.72, which would imply a slight overvaluation after the earnings release.

Looking at the Graham Model, we come across similar insights. Considering an EPS of $3.54 again, we need to estimate a long-term growth rate for it. I consider 5% year-over-year growth to be a reasonable baseline scenario. This is in line with the 10-year EPS growth rate. Note that if we use shorter periods, such as the 5-year average, we would fall into the trap of seeing growth boosted by a smaller base. Something like the post-pandemic/FRC acquisition recovery.

See how this plays out with different growth rates:

  • 5% (historical scenario): Intrinsic value of $57.93. Using SM of 25% we have a PT of $43.45;
  • 8% (scenario projected by Cheesecake Factory): Intrinsic value of $76.72. Using SM of 25% we have a PT of $57.54;
  • 2.5% (catastrophic scenario): Intrinsic value of $42.48. Using SM of 25% we have a PT of $31.71.

Considering all these models I just made, I believe Cheesecake Factory is close to its fair value based on FY 2025 earnings estimates. Of course, I could be wrong if it manages to beat its guidance, and perhaps it will do so with the help of its satellite concepts.

However, I prefer to wait for a market bottom to buy in the high $40s. If you are a Cheesecake Factory shareholder, I would definitely not recommend selling here. FRC concepts can add value over the long term, and that may not be fully priced in, unlike your Cheesecake Factory concept.

Now, if I am a buyer in the high $40s, I believe a PT between $53 to $56 is appropriate here.

I recommend 'Hold' here. I would love for it to drop a little more so I can buy at a bottom. Who knows, maybe we'll find one right there? I'll be waiting!

data.title: LVMH: Not Invited To The Recovery Party
data.description:
LVMH (OTCPK:LVMHF) (OTCPK:LVMUY), typically viewed as a leader in the luxury space, appears to be losing its dominant position in the sector.

In the recently announced fourth quarter, organic growth was a boring 1%, with little acceleration, while peers like Richemont (OTCPK:CFRHF) and Hermes (OTCPK:HESAF) have returned to double-digit growth.

What's going on underneath the expansive LVMH hood? Let's find out.

Introduction To LVMH & Luxury Tiers

LVMH, owner of brands like Louis Vuitton, Dior, Loro Piana, Tiffany, Hennessy, and many more, for years played the role of the luxury bellwether. After a sector-wide surge following the pandemic, we started seeing the different cohorts of the sector beginning to break, one by one.

First, it was the "high-end" group, with names like Capri Holdings (CPRI), and Burberry (OTCPK:BURBY) falling. Then, it was the "aspirational luxury" names, including the likes of Kering (OTCPK:PPRUF), Prada (OTCPK:PRDSF), Moncler (OTCPK:MONRF), and Richemont. Lastly, it was ultra-luxury names that saw a bit of a slowdown, with the main example being Hermes.

For quite some time, LVMH was in its own tier, somewhere between ultra-lux and aspirational. It was able to outperform other aspirational names through diversification, and extremely strong managements, that maintained desirability. However, it seems like recently LVMH is losing ground, coming in fourth during the recent luxury down cycle.

In a series of articles, I accompanied the company's journey from a clear leader to a laggard, which has led me to maintain a 'Hold' rating for the last few quarters. After the recent full-year report, it's time to update our thesis.

Fourth Quarter Results Put A Close On A Lackluster 2024

Revenues in the fourth quarter were €24.0 billion, flat compared to last year, or up 1% on a constant currency basis. This caps off an underwhelming year in terms of growth, as annual sales of €84.7 billion were down 1.7%, or up 1% organically.

Geographically, we saw slowdown across the board in 2024 compared to 2023, and the quarterly cadence saw United States and Europe exit the year at highs of 3% and 4%, while Asia was at 10%, and Japan ended at lows of 8%.

Looking at segments, we see a concerning picture in my view, as we can note that the acceleration in Q4 was primarily in Watches & Jewelry and Selective Retailing.

This strengthens my view that the gifting and special occasions part of the luxury sector is much stronger than personal style. And, Selective Retailing is primarily Sephora, a great business, but not really luxury and less than 8% of group profits.

The weakness in growth, a worse sales mix, and FX headwinds, all drove a 14% Y/Y decline in operating profit in 2024, with a much bigger decline in the second half of the year, giving me pause for the exit rate into 2025.

Unfamiliar Underperformance From LVMH

2024, for the most part, was the toughest year for luxury companies since Covid, and probably the sector's worst year that didn't have a major outlier event in a long time.

Typically, LVMH rises above industry woes, through a differentiated brand portfolio, and a unique management strategy that drives top-tier creative and marketing. However, in 2024, that wasn't the case.

LVMH is trailing Richemont for the first time in many years, and the gap from Hermes is the widest it's been. Critically, LVMH is even more behind based on the fourth-quarter numbers.

LVMH organic growth in Q4-24 was 1%, against Hermes's 18% and Richemont's 10%. Both competitors showed significant acceleration in the quarter, while LVMH only slightly improved.

I do believe Richemont benefitted from its higher exposure to special occasions and gifting, and Hermes is now in a clear league of its own. Also, the gap from Kering remained quite large.

Still, LVMH lost its spot as the inbetweener to Richemont, and it has significant catching up to do in order to justify its premium over the rest of the "struggling" group.

Valuation

At today's valuation, LVMH trades at 25 times forward earnings, based on consensus estimates for 6% revenue growth and 12% EPS growth next year. This would require a meaningful acceleration in organic growth, an easing of FX headwinds, and a 100 bps improvement in profit margins. Possible, but ambitious.

Both on a trailing and a forward basis, LVMH's valuation doesn't reflect a weaker position in the sector. In fact, it's right in line with its historical average.

That said, the entire sector is at peak valuations, with Hermes close to 60x, Richemont at 28x, and Kering at 25x, albeit it's based on supposedly trough earnings.

Conclusion

Unlike previous luxury sector downturns, this time, LVMH wasn't able to rise above the pack, and in fact, it saw competitors like Richemont and Prada take its position, while the gap from Hermes widened.

Looking ahead, it's important to note LVMH's huge scale, exposure to non-luxury categories, and weakness in the Wines & Spirits sector.

The way I see it, the path to see upside from the current valuation goes through meaningful acceleration in growth, as I don't see the multiple expanding further as long as LVMH continues to lag competitors.

Because the growth acceleration path remains uncertain, I reiterate LVMH as a 'Hold'.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Sprouts Farmers Market: Could Benefit From A Shift In Consumer Behavior (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Since my previous Sprouts Farmers Market (NASDAQ:SFM) article published a year and a half ago, the stock has returned a 345% return driven by an increase in growth rates, higher margins, and a multiple expansion.

Sprouts Farmers Market is reducing its outstanding shares at a fast pace and increasing margins could expand valuation multiples.

Source: Tomas Riba, "Sprouts Farmers Market: New Store Format Will Increase ROIC", August 2023.

Although the fundamentals look better than ever, the current valuation multiples might be overoptimistic, causing the recent drop in share price (-15% after earnings release). The guidance provided by the management implies softer revenue growth in 2025.

In this article, apart from updating my expected growth and analyzing the recently published results for Q4 2024, I will review if the company is still compelling from a risk/reward perspective.

The Investment Thesis

My SFM investment thesis is based on a secular tailwind related to an increasing demand for healthier food, a growth in store count, higher returns on capital, a margin expansion, and a lower inventory.

While the company is performing as expected from a financial perspective, the valuation has increased by over 3x, which reduces its ability to conduct further buybacks.

Company Overview

Sprouts Farmers Market covers a particular niche in the grocery retail market related to healthy products and what they call a "lifestyle-friendly" assortment (46% of its products are organic).

The company operates 440 stores in 24 states that are sourced from farmers and distributors. SFM also has its own brand that is already generating 23% of revenues (up from 16% in 2021).

Since produce is one of the keys to SFM's offering (20% of the store’s selling square footage), the company is constantly looking to improve its distribution network to ensure distribution centers are close to its stores (80% are within 250 miles from a DC).

One of the key points of my SFM investment thesis is the decrease in store size, which I expect to increase returns on capital and reduce lease and capital expenditures spending.

The average store size is 28,000 sq feet (reduced from 30,000 sq feet in 2019), but the new store format has a size of between 21,000 and 25,000 sq feet, which will reduce the average store size over the years.

Apart from allocating part of its FCF to increasing the store count, SFM is investing in technology for inventory management, e-commerce, and customer personalization.

E-commerce sales (generated through its website, the Sprouts app, DoorDash, Uber Eats, and Instacart) already represent 14.2% of SFM's revenues, with a positive trend and room to grow further.

One of the most positive developments in 2024, I believe, is the testing of the Sprouts Rewards loyalty program, which is expected to be completely implemented between the last months of 2025 and the beginning of 2026.

This would increase SFM's revenues since 24% of transactions are currently linked to a Sprouts account, but they represent 34% of revenue, meaning that engaged customers have higher average tickets. If the number of loyal customers increases, I believe it should have a positive effect on revenues.

Speed up in Q4 2024

Sprouts Farmers Market just released its Q4 2024 and full-year results, beating estimates in both revenues and earnings per share (EPS) by 1.6% and 7.5%, respectively.

While consensus was expecting EPS for Q4 to be $0.73 per share, the company delivered $0.79 per share (a 61% increase).

Growth in same-store revenue has accelerated from 8.4% last quarter to 11.5% and the company opened 12 new stores (2.8% increase in store count), compared to 9 new openings in Q3 2024. Net sales increased its growth rate to 18% YoY, compared to a 14% growth rate in the previous quarter.

E-commerce sales have been one of the most positive developments during Q4, with revenues growing at a 37% rate.

The company has finished the quarter with a zero debt balance in its revolving credit facility, a $265M cash balance, and repurchased 790,000 shares at an average price of $126 per share.

Solid Long-Term Trend

From a longer time-frame perspective, I believe SFM's fundaments look better than ever.

Increasing margins

Operating and net income margins are increasing and there is more room for future growth since the company has gone from having interest expenses to getting interest income on its balance sheet cash. Long-term debt has declined from 2.86x Net Income in 2018 to zero in 2024.

Operating expenses should decline as costs incurred in store closing decrease when the new store format represents a higher percentage compared to total stores. Now 75 of the 440 stores have the new format, or 17% (up from 10% in 2023), and all new openings planned for 2025 should increase the percentage to 23%.

Return on Capital

To calculate returns on invested capital, I use a slightly different approach than the one that SFM uses in its annual reports.

The reason for doing so is that the use of Net Operating Profit After Tax (NOPAT) excludes certain components such as interest income/expenses, asset writedowns, merger and restructuring costs, and legal settlements, among others. On the other hand, the traditional ROIC approach includes capital leases, which, I believe, is not accurate since the company hasn't spent this capital to acquire the assets they are using.

With this said, I take into account that capital leases increase the company's leverage ratios.

To calculate return on invested capital, I exclude capital lease obligations from invested capital and use net income instead of NOPAT.

As shown in the chart above, returns on invested capital have been showing a positive trend since the company reduced its store format.

Make America Healthy Again

The newly appointed president of the U.S. has established the Make America Healthy Again Commission with the goal of reducing chronic diseases, by exploring fresh thinking on nutrition, physical activities, and a healthy lifestyle. One of the four main policies directives has been stated as follows:

Work with farmers to ensure that U.S. food is healthy, abundant and affordable.

Source: The White House. February 13, 2025.

If the government places policies towards making fresh food more affordable, SFM could significantly benefit, taking into account that, according to a Statista survey conducted in 2023, price is the main barrier to eating healthy food.

While it is hard to find information regarding growth rates in different segments of the food market, Grand View Research expects the U.S. healthy food market to grow at 10% CAGR from 2024 to 2030, compared to the 4.8% for packaged food (snacks, beverages, prepared meals, bakery, and dairy).

Expected Growth

For 2025, the management expects the positive trend to continue with 10.5-12.5% revenue growth (~5.5% from comparable store growth and ~6% from store growth). At current prices, the company would reduce approximately 1.5% of shares outstanding and allocate $240M into capital expenses.

Taking into account the guidance provided and the expected 22.6% EPS growth, the margin expansion should continue.

As the company becomes bigger, I don't expect store count to grow above 10%, but I believe they can keep up with the 6-8% growth rate they delivered over the last years. SFM is only present in 24 states and still has room to grow in existing and new markets.

There are already 110 approved new stores and nearly 70 executed leases in the markets the company is already present in, and management is expecting to enter the Midwest and Northeast states in 2027.

Valuation

In my base case scenario, I take management's word on same-store growth and assume a slightly higher increase in store count for the next year. For the following years, I expect a decline in store count growth and comparable growth to decrease inflation rates.

While this is conservative compared to the Grand View Research previously mentioned, the company doesn't have pricing power and an eventual slowdown of the economy could limit its growth rates.

I am accounting for an expansion in operating margins because of the reasons stated before, a tax cut in 2026 which we are yet to see, and an 8% discount rate.

With a long-term growth rate in line with inflation and a 20x LTM FCF exit multiple, my average fair price is $116 per share and I consider the company to be overvalued by around 20%.

While I am downgrading my rating from buy to hold, I don't believe the company is overvalued enough to assign it a sell rating given the strong fundamentals.

To justify the current valuation, on top of the margin expansion and rate cuts, SFM would have to grow its revenues between 14-15% per year during the next 5 years, which, I believe, is unlikely given the challenging comparables.

Risks

Apart from the short-term risk of a decrease in valuation, from a fundamental perspective the main risks are related to an increase in tariffs, its market concentration in California, and a possible slowdown in economic growth

Tariffs

During the latest earnings call, the management addressed the concern regarding an increase in tariffs on imports.

The majority of food that we sell comes from the United States. We've got some dynamics around our fresh produce business from Central and South America. Exactly what's going to happen to tariffs, we've got no idea of that, and that's something that we will watch and we're ready to move with it. We'll work very closely with our partner growers. We'll work very closely with the opportunity to change sourcing if we need to.

Source: Jack Sinclair (CEO). SFM Q4 2024 Earnings Call.

While the company mainly sources its products from the U.S., I do believe tariffs could have an impact on prices even if they are not applied to food. As an example, an increase in inputs used by farmers could be translated into SFM.

Since the company doesn't have any pricing power, in my opinion, it would have to assume lower gross margins.

Market concentration

As the company expands its store count and enters new states, the market concentration is expected to decrease. But for now, 34% of its stores and 33% of its distribution centers are located in California.

During the last quarter, the team members located in that area have been dealing with the floods in California, the Los Angeles wildfires, and the airplane crash near a Philadelphia store.

While these are, I hope, one-time events, it is important to acknowledge that the company's store base is not big enough to dissipate the one-time events' effects on the overall results.

Economic slowdown

Even though the company is enjoying the tailwinds of a growing market, the affordability of its products has a significant effect on consumer behavior given the elasticity of the demand and the existence of many competitors.

In the event of an economic slowdown, which sooner or later will happen, I believe SFM would be one of the companies negatively affected since many consumers would look for more affordable options.

While it is hard to predict the timing, I prefer to own a bit more defensive companies that are not as impacted by an economic slowdown due to low demand elasticity or high pricing power.

Final Thoughts

Sprouts Farmers Market's execution is on a good track to continue the business expansion at double-digit rates.

The company still has room to expand in existing and new markets, margins should continue increasing due to lower CapEx, higher interest income, and scale benefits. Also, I believe the company is enjoying the tailwinds of a growing market that could be boosted by the Make America Healthy Again Commission if the government places policies towards making fresh food more affordable.

Despite the positive outlook from a fundamental perspective, I acknowledge some risks, and the current price might be too optimistic compared to my fair price of $116 per share.

For those reasons, I am downgrading my rating from buy to hold.
